mcqs.question: In the Bully Algorithm, which process becomes the new leader after a leader failure?

options.text: The process with the smallest ID
The process with the largest ID
The process that detected the failure

mcqs.correctAnswer: The process with the largest ID
mcqs.explanation: The Bully Algorithm always elects the process with the highest priority (largest ID) as the new coordinator. This ensures deterministic selection.

mcqs.question: What is the message complexity of the Bully Algorithm in worst case?
mcqs.explanation: In worst case, the Bully Algorithm can generate O(n²) messages when elections cascade from the lowest to highest process.

mcqs.question: In the Ring Algorithm, how are processes organized?
mcqs.explanation: The Ring Algorithm arranges processes in a logical ring where each process communicates only with its neighbor.

mcqs.question: What type of messages does the Ring Algorithm use for election?
mcqs.explanation: Processes pass election messages (containing candidate IDs) clockwise around the ring until the highest ID is identified.

mcqs.question: What happens when a process receives an election message with a higher ID than its own in the Ring Algorithm?

options.text: It adds its own ID to the message

mcqs.explanation: When a process receives a higher ID, it simply forwards it, waiting to see if any higher ID exists in the ring.

mcqs.question: Which algorithm is more suitable for systems with frequent leader failures?
mcqs.explanation: Ring Algorithm has lower message complexity O(n) and handles failures more gracefully by bypassing failed nodes.

mcqs.question: What is the primary drawback of the Bully Algorithm?

options.text: It doesn't work in asynchronous systems

mcqs.explanation: The Bully Algorithm generates O(n²) messages in worst case, which can overwhelm the system with many processes.

mcqs.question: How does the Bully Algorithm detect leader failure?
mcqs.explanation: Processes assume leader has failed when they don't receive expected messages (like heartbeats or OK responses) within a timeout period.

mcqs.question: What is sent after the leader is elected in the Bully Algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: After election, the new leader broadcasts a coordinator message to inform all processes that it has become the new leader.

mcqs.question: In the Ring Algorithm, when does a process declare itself as the leader?

options.text: When it receives its own ID back in the message

mcqs.correctAnswer: When it receives its own ID back in the message
mcqs.explanation: A process becomes the leader when the election message (containing the highest ID) circulates back to the process that originated it.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the Ring Algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: The Ring Algorithm takes O(n) time since the message needs to make at most one full rotation around the ring.

mcqs.question: Which assumption is NOT required for the Bully Algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: While the Bully Algorithm works best in synchronous systems, it can be adapted for asynchronous systems with appropriate timeout mechanisms.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of OK messages in the Bully Algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: OK messages are sent in response to election messages to prove the sender is alive and prevent unnecessary elections.

mcqs.question: In a system with 5 processes (IDs 1-5), process 5 fails. Which process will become the new leader using Bully Algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: When process 5 fails, the highest remaining ID (process 4) will be elected as the new leader using the Bully Algorithm.

mcqs.question: What is reconfiguration in the context of Ring Algorithm?

options.text: Bypassing failed nodes in the ring

mcqs.correctAnswer: Bypassing failed nodes in the ring
mcqs.explanation: When a process detects its neighbor has failed, it reconfigures the ring by removing the failed node and connecting to the next alive node.

mcqs.question: What invariant must leader election algorithms maintain?

options.text: At least one leader always exists
Exactly one leader exists at any time
The oldest process is always leader
The leader must have the lowest ID

mcqs.correctAnswer: Exactly one leader exists at any time
mcqs.explanation: The fundamental invariant of leader election ensures no partition creates multiple leaders (split-brain) or no leader at all.

mcqs.question: Which of these is a real-world application of leader election?
mcqs.explanation: Distributed databases use leader election to coordinate transactions, manage locks, and ensure consistency across replicas.

mcqs.question: What happens if multiple elections are initiated simultaneously in the Bully Algorithm?

options.text: The highest ID wins all elections

mcqs.correctAnswer: The highest ID wins all elections
mcqs.explanation: When multiple elections occur, the process with the highest ID will win and become the coordinator, resolving conflicts naturally.

mcqs.question: What type of failures can leader election algorithms handle?
mcqs.explanation: Leader election algorithms are designed to handle various failures including leader crashes, network partitions, and process failures.

mcqs.question: In the Ring Algorithm, what information does each election message contain?
mcqs.explanation: Each election message contains the current highest ID encountered. When the message returns to initiator, the highest ID becomes the leader.
